claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with: **Do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me how many children you have and their ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children. My eldest, Blake, is 14 and lives with me at home. My younger one, Alex, is 4 but doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand you have two children - Blake (14) who lives with you, and Alex (4) who doesn't live with you.

Let me ask a few questions to assess eligibility for both children.

First, regarding **Alex (your 4-year-old who doesn't live with you)**: Could you tell me who Alex currently lives with and why Alex doesn't live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent, my ex-partner. We separated a while back and Alex stayed with them, but I still help out and support Alex financially.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. Since Alex lives with your ex-partner, that's an important factor.

Let me ask: **Is your ex-partner claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** Or do you know if they are receiving Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, my ex-partner isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex. That's partly why I'm looking into whether I can claim it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is helpful information.

Now, even though Alex doesn't live with you, there are circumstances where you might still be eligible. Let me ask: **Do you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit rate towards Alex's upkeep each week?** 

For context, the Child Benefit rate is approximately £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, and £16.95 per week for additional children. So in this case, since Alex would be considered in the claim, do you contribute at least around this amount weekly toward Alex's care and upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's upkeep. I help with clothes, food, and other things Alex needs. I give £50 a week to support Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's very helpful to know. Since you're contributing toward Alex's upkeep, let me ask another important question:

**Does Alex spend any time staying with you?** For example, does Alex visit and stay overnight with you at all, or is Alex exclusively living with your ex-partner?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is exclusively living with my ex-partner. They don't stay overnight with me - I don't have regular visits like that at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now let me ask a few questions about **Blake, your 14-year-old who lives with you**:

**Is Blake currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?** (This would be things like secondary school, sixth form, or college - not university degree courses)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is in secondary school - just regular school for their age.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that's helpful. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Are you or Blake currently living in the UK?** And are you (or your partner if you have one) responsible for Blake - meaning you're the one looking after Blake and making decisions about their upbringing?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester. And yes, I'm responsible for Blake. Blake lives with me and I look after them and make all the decisions about their care and upbringing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one final important question:

**Do you meet the immigration and residence requirements?** Specifically:
- Are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status/indefinite leave to remain in the UK, or do you have the right to reside in the UK (for example, through EU settled status or other qualifying immigration status)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm a UK citizen, so that's not an issue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## My Assessment

Thank you for answering all my questions. Based on the information you've provided, here's my determination of your Child Benefit eligibility:

### **For Blake (14 years old): ✓ You ARE eligible**

Blake meets all the criteria for Child Benefit:
- Blake is under 16 and in full-time secondary school education
- Blake lives with you in the UK
- You are responsible for Blake's care and upbringing
- You are a UK citizen with the right to reside in the UK

**You can claim Child Benefit for Blake.**

### **For Alex (4 years old): ✗ You are NOT eligible**

Unfortunately, even though:
- You contribute £50 per week toward Alex's upkeep (which is more than the Child Benefit rate)
- Your ex-partner is not claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- You clearly support Alex financially

The key issue is that **Alex lives exclusively with your ex-partner and does not spend any nights staying with you**. Under UK Child Benefit rules, when a child lives with someone else, generally only the person the child lives with can claim Child Benefit. For you to claim when a child lives elsewhere, the child would typically need to spend at least some regular time with you (at least 52 nights per year, or roughly one night per week on average).

### **What you should do:**

You should apply for Child Benefit for Blake. Your ex-partner should consider claiming Child Benefit for Alex, as they are the person Alex lives with.

reasonings: Blake is 14 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, is in full-time secondary school education, and you are responsible for Blake's care and upbringing. You are a UK citizen meeting the residence requirements. Blake meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Alex is 4 years old (under 16) but does not live with you - Alex lives exclusively with your ex-partner with no overnight stays with you. While you contribute £50 per week toward Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit rate), and your ex-partner is not claiming Child Benefit for Alex, the key issue is that Alex does not live with you and does not spend time staying with you. Under UK Child Benefit rules, when a child lives with someone else, only the person the child lives with can usually claim Child Benefit. The contributing toward upkeep provision generally requires that the child also spends some time with you (at least 52 nights a year or one night per week on average). Since Alex is exclusively with your ex-partner with no overnight stays, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a UK citizen living in Manchester with responsibility for two children. Blake (14) lives with the claimant and attends secondary school, meeting all eligibility criteria. Alex (4) lives exclusively with the claimant's ex-partner with no overnight visits to the claimant. Despite the claimant contributing financially to Alex's upkeep and the ex-partner not claiming Child Benefit, the claimant cannot claim for Alex because Alex does not live with them and does not stay with them. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake only.

Alex is 4 years old (under 16) but does not live with you - Alex lives exclusively with your ex-partner with no overnight stays with you. The contributing toward upkeep provision generally requires that the child also spends some time with you (at least 52 nights a year or one night per week on average). Since Alex is exclusively with your ex-partner with no overnight stays, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (14) lives with the claimant and attends secondary school, meeting all eligibility criteria. Alex (4) lives exclusively with the claimant's ex-partner with no overnight visits to the claimant. Despite the claimant contributing financially to Alex's upkeep and the ex-partner not claiming Child Benefit, the claimant cannot claim for Alex because Alex does not live with them and does not stay with them. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake only.
